data.title: Lamb Weston: Restructuring Plan Could Make It A Takeover Target
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lamb Weston (NYSE:LW) posted their Q1 2025 fiscal results in April 2025, and I must say their Q1 performance was nothing short of optimistic. The company exceeded both its top-line and bottom-line in comparison to analyst consensus estimates. While their earnings decreased to $263 Mn y/y, their net revenue came in strong compared to Q1 2024. Mike Smith, the company's CEO highlighted that the strong performance in the 1st quarter was driven by increased volume, delivering a $55 Mn cost savings due to the restructuring plan, the Lapped 2024 ERP implementation, and the potato write-off impact. The CEO also reaffirmed the company's 2025 outlook of attaining net revenues of between $6.35 Bn and $6.45 Bn. Despite some expected headwinds from quick service restaurant (QSR) traffic, I believe this may be a good opportunity to jump in at a cheap valuation.

The Restructuring plan

LW announced a restructuring plan during the release of their earnings. This plan includes closing down some of the older factories, together with the temporary curtailment of certain production lines. With this restructuring, LW expects to address the supply chain imbalance in North America, better manage its factory utilization rates, generate a $55 Mn in pre-tax savings and a $100 Mn decrease in its capital expenditure. As far as I am concerned, this immediate restructuring will see Lamb Weston's financials turnaround, improve its profitability, and operational efficiency, as noted by the CEO:

These actions are proactive steps designed to improve our operating efficiency, profitability and cash flows, while also positioning us to continue to make strategic investments to support our customers and create value for our stakeholders over the long-term.

2024 Margin Pressure

In 2024, Lamb Weston was facing margin pressure due to a decrease in demand, lower consumption worldwide, and increasing inflation. High restaurant prices were weakening demand for its products. And, analysts had begun to lose confidence in the stock, especially when the share price dropped more than 27% against the S&P500 +22% over the same period.

LW had been experiencing volatile swings during the earnings period, of between -17% to -34% down moves. It is the reason why LW decided to walk away from low margin clients, as mentioned by a company executive. The company thought they had a higher chance of improving their top-line while dealing with higher margin QSR clients. Unfortunately, some of the QSR clients reneged due to slowing restaurant traffic. This led to the company taking a huge potato write-down, which reduced their volume by over 27%. In addition, while all these activities were happening, LW was also in the process of acquiring 4 new plants with a combined capacity of 1.2 Bn lbs. While it wasn't clear how much was paid for these plants, due to late disclosure, investors took their shot again and exited the stock, further causing the share price to plummet.

The Jana Effect

Later in 2024, Jana Partners had built a 5% stake in LW and had teamed up with an agricultural investment firm, Continental Grain. The activist investor only wanted two things for LW: to improve its operations and capital allocations strategy. With a significant stake in the company, the investor could nominate directors to the company's board. Jana believes that LW could be a takeover target for larger food companies. But, LW needs to sort out their mess, improve their operational efficiency, move into profitability, and address their capital allocation strategy.

The Wake-up call

Later in 2024, LW announced cost-cutting measures, including the shutting down of some of their processing facilities and trimming down their workforce. Perhaps, I believe it was the wake-up call needed after Jana and Continental Grain came in, hence the need to implement a restructuring plan. Lamb's net sales have improved by 1% to $1.65Bn. Capital expenditure for 2025 is expected to decrease from $992Mn in 2024 to $750Mn.

The reaffirmation of their 2025 outlook means that the company expects net sales of between $6.6 Bn to $6.8 Bn by the end of Q4 2025. Additionally, there is an anticipated reduction of their capital expenditure by over $100 Mn and an adjusted diluted EPS of $4.15 to $4.35 per share.

The Opportunity

LW is currently trading at more than half its historical EV/EBITDA (TTM) range. (10x vs. 20x to 25x). I believe that the majority of the downside is even priced at a 36% bounce off its July 2024 bottom. In my opinion, LW is cheaply valued, as its shares are trading at a 36% discount from their 2024 peak. As we have seen, there are many variables that led to the sell-off in 2024. However, as I would think, many of these variables are now justified.

In as much as there are other major factors that LW can't control (e.g., crop yield, weather), processors have continued taking prices consistently while demand continues to shift into the upper right. As LW continues with its restructuring plan, its plan of increasing high-margin QSR clients will definitely create value for its stock.

LW's Potential Risk

LW's biggest risk is another low-demand period for their products. As we saw, in 2024, LW was faced with challenges related to demand for potatoes and huge write-offs that decreased the volume by 27%. A distorted crop yield is also a risk worth considering, as it would affect both volume and price, and this would affect net sales in the long run. As a mitigation plan, the management reaffirmed to investors that it was in the final stages of the 2025 crop negotiations. With the negotiations near completion, I would expect a single-digit decline in price, therefore increasing volume and eventually a growth in top-line.

Final Thoughts

Lamb Weston is a company that has faced investors' backlash, especially in 2024. The unknown plant acquisition, high customer renegation at the last minute, and the huge potato write-off certainly explain the stock price performance over the last 12-month period. Despite all that, the company has managed to work around most of its previous issues with the most recent restructuring. And certainly, Q1 2025 exhibits hope. The current trajectory that the company has taken should enable it to regain its lost glory and even become a takeover target, as Jana Partners had thought of. Jana's hand in the company is also a catalyst to look out for, and its partnership with Continental Grain and Alix Partners. While we have not witnessed the full potential of LW restructuring, should the management be successful in fixing the company's operational efficiency, capital allocation, and regaining their market share, this could be a great stock turnaround.

data.title: Investing During A Market Correction
data.description:
Join Alpha Pick's today for more insights from Steven Cress!

This video’s transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and informational purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone. It's Daniel Snyder from Seeking Alpha. Welcome to probably the most important webinar we have ever done in the past few years that we've been doing this. Today, I am joined by the all-stars, Steven Cress, here from Seeking Alpha, VP of Quantitative Strategies, as well as Francesca Friday, who also works with Steve on the Quant team. And we are going to dive through everything macro, what's going on with the markets, as well as what is the investment approach going forward that can help you probably sleep a little bit more soundly at night.

Before we dive in, let me go ahead and get our quick legal disclaimer out of the way. We are not advising you personally concerning the nature, potential, value, or suitability of any particular security. You alone are solely responsible for determining whether any investment, security, or strategy, or any product or service is appropriate or suitable for you based on your investment objectives and personal and financial situation.

This presentation is for information purposes only. Content is presented as of the date published or indicated and may be superseded by future events. It represents my opinions, Francesca's opinions, and Steven's opinions, which may not reflect the views of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Past performance is no guarantee of future results, and Seeking Alpha is not a licensed security dealer, broker, or US Investment adviser, or investment bank.

Now with that out of the way, Steve, there's a lot of uncertainty out there. You've been calling this for a while, but before we dive in, for the people that may be joining us for the first time, why don't you give them a little quick background about yourself?

Steven Cress: Daniel, thank you so much for organizing this today. Highly important, in-light of all the moves that are happening, a lot of fear and anxiety out there, and we're hoping we could address that today. And I have been in the markets for quite a long time, almost 35 years in the financial markets. So, I do have a fair amount of experience through highs and lows. My background has really been, mostly in brokerage firms and asset management. I've been a portfolio manager, headed up a prop trading desk at Morgan Stanley in Quant Strategies for 13 years. That was the bulk of my career. I also had a hedge fund that I founded in Quant Strategies, and I started a fintech company that was acquired by Seeking Alpha about six years ago, which for lack of a better description was one of the first versions of AI for a Robo advisor and picking stocks.

So, that's a little bit about my career. For those who are familiar with me, I've been writing for Seeking Alpha for a long time, and I run the Quant models and Alpha Picks for Seeking Alpha. And I'm joined by our Quant Writer, Francesca Friday, who joined us from the Financial Times. Francesca, tell us a little bit about yourself as well.

Francesca Friday: Hey. Thanks for having me, Daniel and Steve. So, I joined the Seeking Alpha team late last year from a place called Merger Market, where I was a Senior Private Equity Reporter. So, that's where a lot of my background is covering the private markets. I also wrote the FT's award winning newsletter Due Diligence, and that covers topics from M&A to private equity to hedge funds. So, I'm really excited to be a part of the team and diving a little bit more into the public markets with Quant.

SC: Thank you so much. And I'd say with Daniel Snyder who always has his finger on the pulse of the markets, this is a great trio to be having this discussion today. So, I've had a mantra for quite a while. Many markets, many presentations like this, keep calm and carry on. For those who know myself and Daniel, we've had quite a few webinars like this where the markets have been rolling off quite considerably. And this consistently has been our mantra, and it is not going to change today. And we actually started this mantra most recently in January with a couple articles that I had written and guidance that I had given to the market.

First off though, I want to really start with what our Quant vision is? And that is to eliminate emotional investing and uncover top stocks using data driven tactics, which is especially important today because there's a lot of emotion out there and there's a lot of fear. And what we really want people to do is just stay true to a disciplined investment process. Don't really pay attention to the talking heads. There's a lot going on Bloomberg, on CNBC, on CNN. Everybody's talking about the fall from the financial markets, the tariffs. A lot of uncertainty, a lot of unknown impacts. And really, the best thing that you can do is just stay focused in trying to find good investments based on good financials and good fundamentals. And that's going to be a good portion of our presentation today.

So, our Quant vision, turbulent times and market volatility, turn your weakness into opportunity. And this is a message that I've had time and again, during periods like this. And I think individuals who have heeded that have done quite well by taking market uncertainty and volatility and using it to their favor. It can be your best friend.

So, what we're going to cover today is why Quant? We're going to do a recap of the markets, and we'll obviously be covering what's going on with our approach to investing in markets. First, a little bit about Quant, and what we do. We basically at Seeking Alpha, our Quant Strategy is a GARP oriented strategy. That is growth at a reasonable price. So, we're looking for stocks in essence that have strong fundamentals based on growth, value, profitability, positive analyst EPS revisions, and momentum. Those are our core factors that we look at. And because of the ability of our incredible computer models and the powerful computer processing that we possess, we can take those factors and basically score every investable stock every single day.

So, literally, we provide a directional recommendation on every single stock. And when we do it, we're looking at the metrics for our companies, and we're, going through cash flow, income statements, balance sheets, and hundreds of financial metrics. And we compare the metrics for companies to other companies in the sector. So, this gives us the ability to filter out the weak companies from the strong companies. So, part of what we're doing today in our approach, in this balanced approach to investing during this turbulent period is to look for stocks that possess really good fundamentals, but, of course, heeding what's going on in the markets and trying to help eliminate some of the losses that could occur is to encourage people to invest in stocks that have good fundamentals with dividends as well.

So, the barbell approach is continue to look for companies that have excellent fundamentals irregardless of the dividend, identify those companies, but also identify companies that do pay dividends that have good fundamentals and use that as your balanced barbell approach.

So, we have a pretty good track record at Seeking Alpha with our Quant performance. And if you could bear with me, I'd just really want to go through that. We have a back test that goes back to 2010, which shows what our investments would have been like had you invested in all the Quant Strong Buys. And what's interesting about this chart is the back test actually runs from 2010 to 2019.

After 2019, we have the system up and running, and they are simulated trades, but because we know what factors that we look at, we could identify those stocks historically that possess those factors. And what we do is, we back test trying to identify the factors that have the greatest amount of predictability. So, over time, we have come up with weights. So, when we're looking at the factors such as value, growth, profitability, momentum, and the many underlying metrics for those overall factors, it's not equally weighted. Some factors have greater weights than others. Hence, using that system, we've been able to achieve some outstanding results over a long period of time.

So, if you look back to the initial back test, you would see your return would be 2900%, compared to the S&P that has a return of 458% for the same period. But if you look at the recent history, where we've been up and running starting in 2020 where the trades have been simulated, the performance is still just as good. You could see the Quant Strong Buys were up 347% versus the S&P on the far right up a 135%.

Now, the purpose of showing this is, we actually don't have an investable product. There's not an ETF or a mutual fund of our Strong Buys. The point of showing this is to identify how the concept works well, which is that GARP approach. And using the certain weights that we have on factors, it has presented us with some fantastic opportunities that we continue to repeat over time.

So, as a Premium member or an Alpha Picks member, you have the ability to obtain these stocks that are recommended and have these directional recommendations based on the underlying factors. So, that's how we've achieved such good performance over a long time.

DS: All right, Steve.

SC: We're also…yeah.

DS: I want to jump in real quick. I want you to have your victory lap, right? So, if we get back to what we're talking about today, obviously, we can talk about the Quant system all day, but you've been calling for this market uncertainty for months now. It feels like you've been writing the articles. You've been doing webinars with myself. I mean, we just published the top 10 stocks for April video earlier this week where even you were talking about the barbell approach.

So, if you go to the next slide, we can showcase the work that you've been putting out there. So, I just want to encourage everybody. If you're not already, follow Steven Cress on Seeking Alpha. You've been getting these articles. You know that this environment was set up to be uncertain and volatile like it is today.

SC: And Francesca was kind enough to pull this slide together to showcase that, Daniel. So, trying to be as humble as possible and having assessed the markets for a long time, it was, from my perspective, I was able to tell back in early January that there were problems with the market. And one of the things that I say about our Quant model is it's often like a Richter scale. When we notice that there are problems with the stocks that we have Strong Buys, and, typically, we're identifying the best companies and the best industries and the best sectors. They have the strongest growth rates. They have the best valuation frameworks. They have great profitability, but what tends to happen during these periods before the market turns, our Quant Strong Buys often become very volatile and start selling off. And what we're able to see is that there's a an early rotation where there's a flight of capital, and that flight of capital could be to defensive sectors like consumer staples or utilities or cash or gold. And what's interesting is when this initially happens, and much like a Richter scale will go off, it could take a little time before that earthquake happens. The overall market actually continued to go up, but it was going up as investors were rotating into these different sectors.

So, you could see, I wrote this article back on January 10, The Best Defensive Stocks To Hedge Against Market Uncertainty, and then on January 27, Feeling Bearish. And then, of course, on February 25, Brace For Turbulence. And that was just after the market had peaked, I believe, the S&P 500 at that point, but we saw those signs.

So, now when I looked at the market, it was obvious to see what some of the key signs were that was causing that disturbance. And there was increasing talk of tariffs and there was also a degradation that was starting to occur in earnings, especially with the Magnificent 7 stocks. And I pointed out, in many of these article with the Mag 7 stocks that their year-over-year growth had actually been higher than their forward growth rates. And that was across the board for all seven stocks.

So, obviously, we have the largest seven market cap stocks and their growth rates are starting to slow compared to their year-over-year growth rates. And then you have the position coming out of the administration, with a drumbeat that was getting louder and louder on tariffs, it was obvious to see why there was volatility with many of our Quant Strong Buys early on. And, really, it doesn't matter how good the company's earnings results are or their top line results are. When markets get defensive, it's just a giant sucking sound, and it rotates into defensive sectors, as I mentioned, healthcare, utilities, REITs, consumer staples, cash, gold. And people sell stocks where they made money.

So, it doesn't matter if the fundamentals are good or not, they're trying to raise capital, they're trying to raise cash, and they're trying to allocate into sectors that will offer them some protection irregardless of how good the companies are. And that's exactly what we started to experience in early January and why I was writing about it.

So, I also wanted to highlight I put this slide up in February 23rd. Will there be a 10% correction? So, I highlighted the long-term outlook then predicting the exact likelihood of a 10% stock market correction in 2025 is challenging due to many unpredictable factors. However, history does provide some context. And one of the key bullet points that we had here was between 2002 and 2021, the S&P 500 experienced declines of 10% or more in 10 out of 20 years indicating that such corrections occur approximately every two years. So, the history had shown we were at a point where the bull market had run, and it was entering its third year.

And previously when you went more than two years with markets that had returns in excess of 20%, we found that between 2.25 years and 3.25 years, the market on average would return at that point after a 4.8%, hence that correction that came. That might sound a little bit complex, but basically, the way that the math works is that every couple of years the market has a crash. Okay? And, that crash likely will be a minimum of 10%, and in some cases 20%, and we were really hitting on the cusp of it. So, the timing was perfect for this to actually occur.

I did write a couple articles today that I wanted to highlight, which I think are very timely. One of the article is Tariff-Resistant Investments: 3 Stocks For Trade Turbulence. And the article that I wrote, was 'Trump Bump' Hits A Slump: 8 Stocks For A Downturn. So, if you have the chance and you're on Seeking Alpha, you could look up my name and find these articles. And please do follow me, so you could see some of the articles that I write in the future as well.

So, this brings us to our current period of 2025 and what the market normalization is going to be, just after this incredible rotation that we've been seeing. So, just keeping in mind, in 2023 and 2024, the markets then were dominated by the Mag 7. And you may recall in 2023, the Mag-7 stocks on average were up a 111%. And it was still a difficult environment because in 2023, we had rising rates. We had increasing inflation. So, really, investors had a bit of a rotation to what then was a safe haven or the stocks where that had cash, that were really profitable, that were the Mag 7 stocks.

So, based on the fears of rising rates and inflation, but in combination with the AI revolution, you had the Mag 7 do incredibly well during 2023 and 2024 as well. However, as we're coming into 2025, there's a lot of volatility. We have tariffs. We have economic uncertainty. We've had a lot of executive orders coming out of the White House. We've had changes in the regulatory environment. So, there's been incredible gyrations, and there's also been the beginning of some massive layoffs in the federal government as well. And now at a corporate level, many of the executives and CFOs are facing a lot of uncertainty with the tariffs. So, there's uncertainty what they're going to be doing with their CapEx budgets going forward.

Up until a couple days ago, the Mag 7 stocks were down on average about 6.7%. That has quickly changed. So, that's actually a little bit old. I think right now, the Mag 7 stocks are down on average about 20% at this point. You could see, just a couple days ago, the S&P was down 5.54%. At this point today, the S&P is down over 11%. So, just in the few days that we've been writing these slides, the market has come down tremendously. So, what is, like, the normalization that we're going to most likely see for 2025?

So, as I mentioned, the S&P has pulled back roughly 10% or 11% from its 52-week high, which was at this point about 45 days ago. The Nasdaq, I believe is down almost 20% from its high. So, ignoring the slide here, and that was about a 110 days ago. So, just in the last five days, we've had these huge moves in the market. And the concerns are around tariffs, inflation, corporate earnings deterioration, and looming and increasing fears that a recession may occur. And all these have contributed to the pullback that we see.

And on the back of these fears, investors, obviously, they've been taking profits. They've been selling their stocks that are winning. And, when I say those stocks that have been winning, you could look at the Mag 7 stocks. You could look at a lot of the stocks that we've been recommending over the last year, they have been hit particularly hard in the last couple weeks, despite the incredible results that they've had in terms of their earnings announcements.

So, what has done well, we have seen a shift to consumer staples, as I mentioned, utilities, REITs, healthcare, cash, and gold. And that's how the market plays out during a period of fear, which is exactly what we're experiencing right now.

So, this next slide says, Blind Faith Has a Short Shelf Life. And it's just an example that really just in November and December, Wall Street was rejoicing. Trump won the election. They did announce the aggressive tariff plans, but really at the time, people thought that that would be a tool for negotiation. And that has clearly turned for a tool of negotiation into a reality.

So, at this point, we have fears of stagflation, which are starting to mount. We have other countries that will be matching tariff increase. So, we have basically a tariff where that's occurring. We're probably starting to see a slowdown in economic growth. We'll probably see rising unemployment, and we'll probably see inflation numbers coming in much harder than expected as we go into the next several months. And this is all being factored into the market today, yesterday, the day before. People have been positioning for this.

So, really, with the market being down 5% today, it's almost like an orderly decline in the market. Because a lot of this news has been out there for the last couple weeks, with the events that happened yesterday with the announcements of tariffs, with the bad news being laid out, the market is just absorbing that right now. So, a lot of these moves have already taken place.

And this, I think is a good chart on the right hand side, which just shows you how much consumer confidence has slipped in. This is before yesterday. And you could see the incredible change in delta from consumer confidence in November of 2024 to March 2025 where it dropped to 92.9 from 111. And this is prior to what happened yesterday.

So, we'll probably continue to see consumer confidence slip in the weeks and months ahead, and we'll probably be seeing it on the corporate level as well. And many of the reasons why this is occurring, I've already mentioned.

But what I wanted to show here is, the VIX. And we had a period back, I believe it was August last year. Daniel, is that correct?

DS: Yeah. August.

SC: Where you put up a similar slide and the VIX had surged over 60. And the only time previously to that, well, in recent, like history was during the Great Financial Crisis. We're seeing today the VIX is not up as much as it was during that one gyration or during the Financial Crisis. And I think, the market can take heart to that that this is a bit of an orderly sell off that we're seeing. And, today, we're really seeing, with the movement in interest rates today, investors are going into bonds.

So, bonds are going up a bit. Yields are going down. People continue to go into cash. People continue to go into consumer staples even today as we're speaking, based on the fears of unemployment rising, economic uncertainty, and what's going to happen with inflation with all these tariffs. But the good news is, we could see that the VIX has not surged to some of those previous levels, which tells you it's a bit of an orderly sell off.

Daniel, I wanted you to tell us about this chart.

DS: Yeah, Steve. Thank you. So, I think this is, like I said, this is probably the most important webinar we've ever done, right? I want to take a second and just remind people what the VIX actually is, what it actually does because it always comes up in conversation. People lose hindsight of. The VIX is a calculation of the option prices, 30 days in the future, calculating the strikes and the implied vol that the market is seeking.

If people are hedged, you don't get these massive shoots that just pop-up like crazy in the VIX, right? So, keep that in mind. Also, you have to remember the historical average of the VIX is around that 20 level. So, when you take that 20 level and start building out standard deviations of what is a normal distribution, your first standard deviation takes you up to about 28 to 30 depending on how you calculate it.

What we're showing here is, I don't know if you all remember the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank. When that happened, we barely got over the 28 level before the market pulled back. That was a moment in time where people were wondering if they need to take cash out of their bank accounts. They were anticipating a failure, and yet the VIX never got above 30, really. Then if you go to the next slide, as you're talking about on August 5, we saw a five standard deviation event on a Monday morning because people didn't know what was going on in the currency market, right?

No nuke got shot in the air. The markets were still open that day. They didn't close. Like, for the VIX to pop, right, there is still a possibility. I'm not going to say there's not a possibility here, but for the VIX to pop, there has to be something unseen that hasn't been hedged by everyone on Wall Street and customers out around the world, right? People knew about the tariffs. So, what Steve was saying. They knew the tariff announcement was coming. This is why, as Steve mentioned, it feels like a controlled sell-off in the market, which is why we only got to about that negative 5% value today, but I just wanted to highlight that. So…

SC: Yeah. I think these were great slides, and I did throw in a little plug there for our Quant system. During the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, our Quant rating system was one of the very, very few systems on Wall Street that had a Sell on Silicon Valley Bank. I don't think there was one other firm out of the big tier banks or the second tier brokerage firms. No one was really calling for a Sell. And our Quant system had a Sell on a number of the banks that actually had to merge or were taken over during that period. So, hats off to the Quant system during that period.

So, I'm going to take us to what we're seeing in terms of a 30-day recap bringing us right into this current period. As you could see, it continues with this rotation. So, looking on the far right, over the last four weeks, the one-month period, we were seeing a move into energy. I think that is sold-off today, so that's probably beginning to disappear. I took this from early this morning. Energy has moved down. Energy stocks have moved down as well, but you could see, the sectors have performed well where utility and consumer staples and to a certain extent, basic materials.

And basic materials are interesting because commodities actually fluctuate quite a bit every year. So, the fact that we have these tariffs that are coming in, at all different levels anywhere from 10% to maybe 30%, people have to bake in a lot of volatility with basic materials and commodity prices. So, that's why you're not going to see that sell-off. And, of course, people are moving into gold and silver as well. So that will impact basic materials, but, of course, if you look at the bottom on the right hand side, just in the four week period, the sectors that have sold-off sharply were the ones that are the best performing sectors for the last two years running. Technology, consumer discretionary, communication services. Those sectors that were the best performing ones have sold-off. So, this is sort of a recap.

And, again, we have a bit of an orderly sell-off today because this has occurred over the last four weeks as rotation. So, the tariffs were not really a huge surprise, hence like the VIX not getting to those previous levels.

Part of the warning that I mentioned that we saw early on in January was, with the discussion of tariffs growing and that sound getting louder and louder, I put together this graph because the market and economists and the economy do not like tariffs. So, you could see on this graph over here and the performance of the S&P 500 the year that tariffs were launched. So, the big year was when Herbert Hoover was President. In 1930, he launched the Smoot-Hawley Tariffs, were actually the tariffs are most similar to the ones that we are seeing today, which were global tariffs. And in 1930, the market fell 28.5%, and that was on the back of the market coming off sharply in 1929. And, of course, that was really driven by an incredible amount of margin that was made available to the market margin calls and then central banks tightening into that period when the market was collapsing.

So, between 1929 and then having the Smoot-Hawley Tariffs in 1930, it was a really harsh period for the market over 2.5 year period there. But the tariffs, had such a negative impact on the markets that there wasn't another President until Richard Nixon, that launched tariffs, and that was during the Nixon Shock. And those were pretty light. So, in the year he launched it, the market was pretty much unchanged. However, I will say, a recession did follow that. And the recession started in 1969, resurfaced through 1973, abated, and then continued again into 1975. So, it was a really bad economic period, 1969 through 1975. Again, tariffs not having a good impact on the market, but George Bush, to a limited extent, had steel tariffs that he announced in 2002, and the market reacted very negatively to that. It was down 23%.

Of course, there were other issues during each of these periods. You have the oil shock in 1970. And, of course, you had the start of the Depression in 1929. And then when George Bush was President, obviously, we had the Iraq war occur and the horrible New York crisis with the World Trade Center that occurred too. So that was a pretty horrific period. So, it's not all tariff related. In 2018, Donald Trump had China and steel tariffs, so very limited compared to the amount of tariffs announced yesterday, but the market fell about 6.2%. And then, of course, you had the pandemic.

So, I'm not sure what it is about tariffs, but they seem to come along with periods in time where there are other events that are not very timely, but on average, during these tariffs, during the first year that they were launched, the S&P fell about 14.5%. So, you're already seeing the Nasdaq probably about off 20% and the S&P 500 close to that now.

Here's a little bit of history as well. We took, 14 major crises that have occurred since 1980, and we have measured the decline. So, what I'm looking at here as the question that I'm getting is, how long could this potentially last? So, since 1980, the markets have entered a correction every 3.2 years. 173 days is the median pullback period. We want to identify the companies that have strong Growth rates and good Valuation frameworks. Because over time, fear fades and the market will return to fundamentals.

So that's why especially in periods like this, you could use this uncertainty as your best friend. And if you identify these stocks now, when the fear settles down and the market does return to fundamentals, you will definitely be rewarded by it.

DS: Rightly said. All right. I'm going to squeeze one more in here, actually, because as I mentioned, we're going to wrap up because we're past 5 o/clock here. But when it comes to us coming on these webinars, I saw this question, it comes up all the time. You're – we're always recommending stocks to pick, and we're always saying, well, okay, it's the Strong Buy, and it's the GARP system. Maybe just take a second to remind people what the GARP system is because I think people are naturally thinking about, well, if you're saying buy this stock now, then the market must be bottoming, right? I think that's the natural thought people have. So when we come here and we say, here's a list of eight stocks today, maybe just give them the compelling explanation of the growth at a reasonable price.

SC: Right. Exactly. So – and we're not saying that the market has bottomed here yet. But what we're saying is the pullback has been significant enough that you probably want to start paying attention to these companies that have incredible fundamentals.

So when we look at GARP, growth at a reasonable price, we're looking for companies that have strong growth rates at a reasonable valuation. And I think really to, like, highlight this. So a lot of people are moving into consumer staples stocks right now. So look at the EPS. The average EPS growth rate for Alpha Picks is 36%, okay? And I highlighted compared to the S&P, it's 13%. But this is a 36% EPS growth rate.

So I'm going to take us to our platform, so you could see what the growth rate is for a sector that people are going to, which is consumer staples. So here we have 36% for Alpha Picks. So I'm going to go to Pepsi, which is actually a stock that we do have in Alpha Picks. But what I want to highlight is I'm going to click on Growth, and we're going to look at the EPS growth rate.

So for the consumer staples sector, the growth rate is 8%, okay? So as an investor, wouldn't you want to be investing in companies that have a growth rate that's 36% as opposed to growth rate that's only 8%? And in the same token, the multiple for the Alpha Picks portfolio is 18x. If we go to the multiple for the consumer staples sector, okay, it's actually on a trailing basis, it's 21x. On a forward basis, it's 19x. So the average P/E for the Alpha Picks portfolio is 18x. It's actually at a discount to the consumer staples sector.

So this is a sector that people are flying to. And the reason why they're flying to it is consumer staple companies tend to have very stable earnings no matter what the environment is. People are still going to need to drink. They're still going to have their beverages. They're still going to have to buy certain foods.

So that's why they tend to go to that, or they'll go to the utility sector because they need to keep the lights on in the house, okay. But you're looking at Growth rates that are much, much lower and Valuations at this point, which are actually more expensive than stocks that we consider attractive.

So, again, in Alpha Picks, we have something – we have about 38 stocks in the portfolio. And of the 38 stocks, the average EPS growth is 36%. The average P/E is 18x. That is actually at a discount to consumer staples with a growth rate that's triple, more than triple than the consumer staples sector.

So, Daniel, hopefully, that answers your question. Daniel, I think maybe we can take a couple of more questions. It's a pretty hectic day. I know we normally like to keep these to an hour, but some people might want to hang on a little bit longer. So ask away with a couple of more questions.

DS: Well, then let's go back to the platform real quick. I got one for you. It came up. So a company like Nike got demolished today off of the tariff news. And I think this is a compelling case that you can maybe walk us through not only the Quant system here, but also focus on the Dividend Grades and kind of give us a look into what you're seeing here.

SC: Okay. Well, from a Quant system, it does have a Hold on the stock, but not all Holds are created equal. So one of the things I could tell you that doesn't look really attractive about Nike is you do have that Valuation Grade at a D, but what is not great is the Growth Grade is an F.

So let's take - it's one click away. You could see these underlying metrics. The reason why Nike has an F for Growth is the year-over-year growth rate is actually negative 7.3% and the forward growth rate is negative 3%.

So the top line growth for this company is negative. If we look at some of the bottom line EBITDA numbers, the EBITDA growth is negative 15% year-over-year and forward, it is negative 13%.

So growth does not look good for this company, and the forward growth rate is negative. So Nike is really not an attractive stock. So when you throw the tariffs on top of it, this was a picture of it, pre-tariffs, okay? Now you throw the tariffs on top of it, Growth just does not look good for a company like Nike, and this is kind of what the Quant system is telling you.

So we like stocks that look good on a GARP basis, growth at a reasonable price. Nike would not fit that because the Growth is in the negative domain, and the Valuation is expensive. So this would not make a Quant Strong Buy.

DS: Thanks for that. So there was a question about the slide you shared earlier about the median time of a market pullback and coming down to August 9 as the date that, you're saying give or take. So they're asking, why not wait until August to invest in stocks? Why start buying stocks now?

SC: Okay. So one of the examples that I showed is we went back to the beginning of 2010. There have been five market pullbacks of 15% at that point. So when the market is down 15%, if you held it for two years, we showed you on average, if you took advantage of the Top 10 Quant Stocks, it was up 117%. The S&P was up 50%.

So when it's down 15%, that's actually a good point to start dipping your toes in the water and start looking for some of these stocks. Particularly, if the market is down 15%, the stocks that are probably the Quant Strong Buys are down even more because a lot of them started coming off in early January and February pretty significantly.

You may recall I said the Quant is like a Richter scale. When we have stocks that we've identified as Strong Buys that are really attractive and that have had good run ups on Momentum, they'll sometimes be the first things that get sold. People will take profits where they've made money as they look to put it into cash or to consumer staples or gold. So chances are a number of the stocks that we like probably have already sold off more than the market.

So using that level for the market as a benchmark at down 15% is usually a fairly good time to start putting your toes back in the water. But, certainly, I'd say be patient. if you have powder on the sideline, just use a little bit of it.

I would especially say if you have stocks that are speculative companies where there are no earnings growth, where the earnings could be negative, you might want to move out of some of those speculative investments, put – and put the money into stocks that have dividends or stocks that have really good fundamentals. This is definitely not a good period for speculative investments.

So you probably have noticed a lot of the Russell stocks, the small-cap stocks have sold off considerably from its 52-week high, and that's because there's so much uncertainty with inflation. With tariffs, it brings inflation. There's always that scarcity value that interest rates are going to have to move up if you have inflation. Small caps don't do well during that period.

So if you have small-cap stocks that have limited profitability, you may want to choose to sell some of those stocks to have some cash to put into the quality stocks.

DS: All right. And one last question here. People are asking the Quant system, how do they know when to sell the stock, right? Because these are all Strong Buys. When do they know to Sell?

SC: Well, when the Quant Grade goes to Sell or a Strong Sell, that's how you know. So a little bit different from what I said early on where the Quant system could often be like a Richter scale. It gives me that that sign, like, I start to see volatilities in our Strong Buy stocks that have performed extraordinarily well. They start selling off. And when they sell off in size, and not size, like, just being one company, but, like, a big breadth of the stocks start to sell off, it notifies me that there's a rotation that's starting to occur. That doesn't mean sell those stocks. That means the fundamentals are actually getting more and more attractive as the stocks are getting cheaper. But for a stock that you buy, as a Quant, when you really want to know to sell it, it's when the directional recommendation changes to Sell or Strong Sell.

DS: Perfect. And I know that's a strategy you use within the Alpha Picks portfolio, right? So maybe you just want to take a second and just kind of tell people about that.

SC: Yeah. So we will sort of end on that. I mentioned the Alpha Picks portfolio. So, investing, it often requires a lot of time. You could be a sophisticated or an engaged investor, or you could be a beginner. Time is often limited. So what we do with Alpha Picks is every month, we take our top two Quant recommendations and we bring it forward.

On the trading date closest to the 1st of the month and the 15th of the month, we send out an article on some of our top Quant stocks. And we started this about a little more than 2.5 years ago, and you could see the performance has been exceptional.

Alpha Picks is up 129% versus the S&P up 48%. And I could say pretty consistently since inception, whether you look at a one-month timeframe or one-year timeframe or six months, it's outperformed. I will say in the last month, that has changed. And since February, a lot of our Alpha Picks stocks have gotten completely clobbered. But, again, it doesn't have anything to do with the results.

So I've grayed out a couple of most of the names here, but we just finished up our recent earnings period. And we saw that 87% of the Alpha Picks stocks beat earnings estimates that were out there from Wall Street analysts versus the S&P 500, where only 75% of the stock had beaten analyst expectations.

So it's a really positive sign, and what you can see here is a list of all the companies with the EPS surprise. So almost every one of them, okay, beat analyst expectations in the most recent quarter and pretty much the same for the top line. Really some of them – two of them were just off minimally. You're talking about companies that make billions of dollars. So to be, like, $9 million off of, like, $3.7 billion is really like a non-event.

So for the most part, these companies really did great off of the last earnings period beating both top and bottom line expectations, but many of these stocks have gotten crushed since February bringing down the returns of Alpha Picks.

So, again, uncertainty can be your best friend. You want to take advantage. You want to invest in companies that have strong growth rates, top line and bottom line, and that's what we bring forward with Alpha Picks. But markets come down. There's nothing we can do about it, but what you can do is take advantage of the opportunity that it presents. And we've shown many examples in this presentation going back to 1929.

For the duration that markets come off, how much they come off, but how much they appreciate in the period following when they've had that decline. And that's really what it comes down to here today is don't let fear get the best of you. Be a disciplined investor, okay, and look to make structural moves at this point where you find stocks with good fundamentals. Because the more the market comes off here, the greater your return is going to be. And it doesn't take that long, okay?

Even during The Great Depression, when the market had its biggest move down 89% in a two-year period, had you invested, one-year later, you would have been up over 100%. And we found the same thing during the pandemic. We found the same thing during the financial crisis, and we're going to find it into the future as well.

DS: Steve, Francesca, we can't thank you enough. Obviously, as I mentioned at the top, probably the most important webinar we have ever done with the markets that we're in. I mean, this has been incredible. Thank you so much for taking the time to put all this data together, walk us through the history, present these stocks, walk us through the metrics, walk us through the platform. There's been a lot of positive comments coming into the chat.

And anybody watching the replay, I hope you learned something as well. Highly encourage you to go follow Steven's profile here on Seeking Alpha to read all of his analysis. Check out Alpha Picks, and we'll see you here soon in the next video.

SC: Thank you so much, everybody.

FF: Thanks, everyone.

Join Alpha Pick's today for more insights from Steven Cress!

data.title: April 2025 Outlook: Calamos International Small Cap Growth Fund
data.description:
By Nick Niziolek, CFA, Dennis Cogan, CFA, Paul Ryndak, CFA and Kyle Ruge, CFA

During the first quarter of 2025, global markets sharply and quickly reflected the possible commencement of an inflection in investor sentiment to favor ex-US markets over the long-preferred US market. After years of US and foreign investors building up exposure to the US market, tariff and fiscal policy developments may be catalysts for capital migration out of the US and into ex-US markets at the margin.

US policies—from both DOGE and tariff perspectives—have introduced sentiment uncertainty into the US economic outlook, potentially indicating a crack in the US-exceptionalism trade. In contrast, in Europe, policymakers have initiated efforts toward increased defense and fiscal spending impulses, in concert with a stronger focus on investment in innovation throughout the continent. Additionally, we continue to see increased policy efforts toward consumer support in China and a refreshed willingness from the leadership to support the country’s most innovative companies. As fiscal spending shifts from the US to abroad, we believe investors are becoming excited about the potential for a pick-up in growth outside the US. (For more, see our recent post, “Overseas Investment Opportunities: US Policy Shifts Awaken the Sleeping Giants.”)

We welcome this environment. Our time-tested investment process is designed to quickly identify companies across an expansive universe that can benefit most from inflections. We then promptly size up our portfolio exposures to these opportunities. The fund remains diversified across a range of cyclical and secular themes, and we are excited that the breadth of opportunities has expanded across the global market, as previously out-of-favor areas of the markets enjoy renewed interest.

In this commentary, we examine some of these dynamics in more detail and provide insight into some of the ways we have positioned the fund to benefit from these changing dynamics.

International small caps. As we enter a more attractive market environment for international equities, we believe international small caps should do particularly well.

As markets recover and earnings inflect (as we are seeing early signs of in Europe), many small-cap companies could benefit most from an economic rebound, given that smaller companies often have more exposure to local economic drivers than their larger global peers. Extensive research and active management will be key to identifying which small-cap companies will benefit to a greater degree from these growth inflections. Given that international small caps are not as well covered in the investment community, we believe our time-tested process and experience investing in international small caps across portfolios will remain an important differentiator. (For more, see our paper, “An Experienced Approach Targets International Small Cap Opportunity.”)

Finally, we believe valuations for international small caps are still attractive relative to US small caps. As shown below, international small caps are trading between one and two standard deviations below the historical average discount to US small caps.

Attractive Valuations in International Small Cap Stocks

MSCI ACWI ex USA Small Cap Forward P/E Discount or Premium vs Russell 2000 Index

Europe. While there have been extended periods when US equities outperformed their European counterparts or vice versa, the current cycle has been exceptional in terms of the length (17 years) and the magnitude of US equity outperformance.

Relative Performance of European Equities Compared to US Equities since 1970 (in USD)

For a long while, we have been on the lookout for a catalyst to arrest this trend, and several catalysts have converged over the past few months that give us confidence that the year-to-date counter-trend rally in European equities is sustainable and may even be the beginning of a new cycle.

As we wrote in our recent post, “Overseas Investment Opportunities: US Policy Shifts Awaken the Sleeping Giants,” “America First” policies ultimately may be the catalyst for overseas equity outperformance, as countries realize that they may no longer be able to rely on the US for export-led economic growth and security. Germany recently passed infrastructure and defense spending plans that we estimate can bring 1%–2% to GDP annually over the next few years and pull the German economy out of recession. As we wrote in our recent post, “In Defense of Higher Spending: Geopolitics Creates Secular Opportunities,” we expect NATO countries will increase defense spending to 3%–5% of GDP. This commitment boosts the growth prospects of many companies, and we are finding many new opportunities for the fund.

Meanwhile, we anticipate Germany’s infrastructure proposal will further widen this opportunity set. We believe transportation, power, energy, education, rails, and ports are all compelling areas of investment focus, and we have identified a range of European industrials and materials companies that we expect to benefit from these new plans.

As these stimulus packages work their way through the economy, European banks are positioned to benefit from increased loan growth and a more favorable regulatory environment. (For more, see our recent post, “European Banks: Hard Work Pays Off.”)

US Dollar. The move in the US dollar has historically been a telling indicator of the relative performance prospects of international versus US stocks. An extended period of a sideways-to-weaker dollar has historically supported higher relative returns for international stocks, as it traditionally encourages global allocation and capital flows into ex-US developed and emerging markets.

During the first quarter, softer US economic data, tariff concerns, and fiscal uncertainty contrasted strikingly with major international economies enacting economic stimulus policies. Against this backdrop, the US dollar began a steady decline lower from a starting valuation position, arguably at its most extended point over the past 50+ years (see figure below).

The US dollar recently traded over two standard deviations above the average versus other currencies

We may not be witnessing a “weak dollar” but, for the first time in a very long time, a “strengthening euro.” Regardless, the change contributed to decisive international equity markets’ outperformance during the quarter. (For more, see our post, “Why the Dollar Inflection Should Not Be Ignored.”)

Major global assets tend not to move in a straight line in one direction. That said, we believe the dollar’s trajectory during the first quarter could be a preview of a sustained tendency for a sideways-to-downward US dollar.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Volkswagen: Losing The Race On Multiple Fronts
data.description:
Volkswagen (VLKPF) (VWAGY) (VWAPY) struggles, and the German auto industry struggles with it. The carmaker has been making the headlines for several months, and none of them was optimistic. The doom and gloom spill over the home of the four-wheeled vehicles with little hope for an improvement.

Volkswagen is not alone. Its competitors suffer as well. However, compared to foreign rivals with a similar scale, the legacy automaker from Wolfsburg is exposed to the EU- and Germany-specific hardships. Although the company is trying to do its best to turn the tide, the economics of the business and the industry remain terrible. No matter what Volkswagen does, it won't change. The whole case for the awfulness of the car industry was made in the article released on March 21, 2023, titled Tesla And Terrible Business Economics, which is still very up-to-date. Now, when the times are hard, it's visible more than ever. Due to its size and importance to the European economy, Volkswagen is a great example of what a business in an industry with terrible economics means. Regardless of how brilliant the management is, the company may keep struggling, just like Volkswagen does these days.

When an industry with a reputation for difficult economics meets a manager with a reputation for excellence, it is usually the industry that keeps its reputation intact.

~ Warren Buffett

Automotive Sector and the European Union

Mario Draghi, an economist and former European Central Bank president, released an insightful document titled The Future of European Competitiveness. His analysis is fact-based, focusing on numbers and data over many years. It's also quite contrary to Brussels's position. In recent years, the EU has emphasized decarbonization and alternative energy sources, regardless of their effect on the industry. However, the paper underscores the importance of innovation and job preservation in Europe, questioning the EU policies. The analysis also covers the automotive sector specifically and points to some of the causes of the EU's eroding competitive advantages in this area. Naturally, it affects its biggest auto manufacturer, Volkswagen.

To get a better overview of how influential the auto industry is in Europe, here are some facts:

  • The automotive industry is responsible for 13.8 million jobs. It accounts for 6.1% of total EU employment.
  • 2.6 million people out of the total 13.8 million are employed directly in vehicle manufacturing. It translates to 8.5% of the EU’s manufacturing workforce.
  • The sector also contributes 8.0% to the total value added in European manufacturing.
  • The auto industry is a relevant branch of many European economies. Germany ranks sixth, and automotive accounts for 11% of the country's total manufacturing.

  • The European car sector drives research and development (R&D). The R&D-related spending constitutes 15% of the industry’s gross value added.

As the list shows, the importance of the automotive industry for the EU economy is undeniable. However, the sector is undergoing a transformation that has been painful for the European economy so far. The shakeup is caused by several factors, such as:

  • the shift in demand from developed to third-world markets,
  • the rise in popularity of electric vehicles (EVs),
  • the digitalization of automobiles and the most recent innovation driver - artificial intelligence [AI],
  • rigorous EU climate policies,
  • tough competition from China.

These are the main aspects that have led to the deterioration of the EU's automotive sector, which started for good last year.

  • Car production was 20% below the pre-pandemic levels in 2024, which amounts to 3 million vehicles.
  • Just last year, suppliers in the automotive sector announced 54,000 job cuts, having totaled 145,000 positions since 2019.

  • In 2000, 18 million cars were manufactured in the EU, corresponding to 31% of total global output. By 2022, this share had decreased to 15%, with production down to 13 million.
  • Meanwhile, China's market share surged from 4% to 27%, meaning 2.0 million and 27.0 million cars, respectively.
  • According to estimates, the production cost of a vehicle is 30% higher in the EU than in China.
  • China is the largest importer of cars to Europe, having scaled from 114,000 vehicles in 2017 to 561,000 in 2022.

While Europe faces higher production costs, technological regression, and deterioration of brand value, China shifts into sixth gear and speeds up. It looks like nothing can save the EU if there is no dramatic shift coming soon. As Mario Draghi's analysis points out:

Chinese OEMs are one generation ahead of Europeans in terms of technology in virtually all domains, including EV performance (e.g. range, charging time, and charging infrastructure), software (software-defined vehicles, autonomous driving levels 2+, 3 and 4), user experience (e.g. best-in-class Human Machine Interfaces and navigation systems), and development time (e.g. 1.5 to 2-year development time, compared to three to five years in Europe).

From the global perspective, the EU and its automakers, among which is Volkswagen, are in a very tough situation. The data and the trends speak strongly against the growth of the automotive sector in Europe. Of course, anything can happen, but the number and the weight of negative developments is overwhelming. It gives little hope for the future of automobiles in the old continent, which seems to be dominated by China.

Crisis at Volkswagen

Germany, the homeland of the automotive industry, is experiencing an earthquake that hit after rumors broke out in September 2024 that 30,000 Volkswagen employees would lose their jobs. On December 20th, 2024, the seventy-hour-long negotiations between the company and the IG Metall unions ended. Considering the acute situation and the need for quick, radical changes to save the stumbling auto giant, the winner of this talk is a Volkswagen employee.

IG Metall is the largest industrial union, gathering 2.2 million members, and it is very influential. It not only represents a substantial part of the Volkswagen workforce, but also its spokespeople have seats on the company's board. That's how they can participate in the decision-making process. As with everything, there are pros and cons. However, a German employee, specifically at Volkswagen, has privileges many could dream of. While benefits, job security, and stability are good for the workforce, they can pose a disadvantage for the employer, especially in hard times.

Job guarantee

In the early 1990s, Volkswagen faced a crisis, and thousands of jobs were on the line. Since IG Metall was already a powerful entity back then, massive layoffs were avoided, and a number of measures were taken to protect the workforce. One of them was a job guarantee policy, which evolved over the years. It gives employees a nearly unbreakable job security. He can't be dismissed for economic reasons. The only possibility of laying the person off is due to violations of the contract.

While the policy protects employees, gives them life stability, and prevents labor strikes, which, on the other hand, could disrupt production, it ties Volkswagen's hands. A recession, a restructuring, or a change in strategy, when a shift in workload or personnel is needed, becomes nearly impossible. The business can't quickly adapt to the evolving industry. It's rigid and dependent. Additionally, the time and effort that goes into negotiations with the unions and finding alternative ways to the most obvious and effective solutions could be spent much more efficiently.

The most recent negotiation talks between IG Metall and Volkswagen resulted in the following major decisions:

  • the job security policy was extended to 2030,
  • the 5% wage increase will be postponed until 2030, and the special flexible working time models will be implemented to avoid layoffs,
  • Some bonus payments will be adjusted (but not necessarily reduced).

Those 35,000 jobs will be cut in a "socially responsible manner," meaning that the employees won't be laid off. They will be released through early retirement offers, voluntary buyouts, or flexible working models.

A striking piece of the negotiations, which wasn't emphasized by the media, is the massive reduction of the technical development at the main Volkswagen location. 4,000 out of 12,000 jobs will disappear from Wolfsburg. Will it be German Detroit? These 4,000 positions would be occupied by highly qualified engineers whose expertise and know-how would strengthen the business at home. It would contribute to in-house innovation and reinforce the brand. Yet, these qualities aren't the priority in times of pursuit of cost reduction.

The picture below shows well how much anger and disappointment the announcement of massive layoffs caused. Is the job reduction over time through early retirement, voluntary buyouts, flexible working models, etc., not just a slowdown of an inevitable agony for the Volkswagen plant in Wolfsburg?

The symbolic tombstone in the picture above reads:

Volkswagen values †09/02/2024

First Volkswagen

then the suppliers

then the elderly care nurses

then the kindergarten caregivers

then the bakers

then the butchers

then the restaurateurs

then the locksmiths

then the electricians...

German Automotive Sector Is In Trouble

A large-scale transformation in the sector, combined with a crisis of the biggest European car manufacturer, affects the whole country. Volkswagen and other OEMs face serious obstacles, and their suppliers felt the pain first. The sentiment in the homeland of automobiles sinks deeper with every announcement of high-scale layoffs, business closures, or branch shutdowns. Since last year, already before the heated negotiations between IG Metall and Volkswagen, notable as well as smaller enterprises got shaken:

  • 08/12/2024 - IAV, a key development partner of Volkswagen, plans to cut 600 jobs while employing 8,000 people worldwide, 6,200 of which are in Germany.
  • 11/22/2024 - One of the largest automotive suppliers, Bosch, plans to cut jobs worldwide. In Germany alone, around 3,800 jobs will be eliminated.
  • 11/26/2024 - thyssenkrupp, a steel producer with 159,000 employees, will reduce 11,000 jobs by 2030.
  • 01/17/2025 - Job cuts began at the major automotive supplier ZF Friedrichshafen. Up to 14,000 positions are planned to be eliminated.
  • 02/18/2025 - Continental, another significant company in the German automotive sector, announced 7,150 job cuts in its future automotive division. An additional 3,000 cuts were just revealed, which in total will exceed 10,000.
  • 02/25/2025 - The production of the famous brake manufacturer Knorr-Bremse in Dresden is being relocated to Poland. 40 jobs will be lost.
  • 03/05/2025 - Bosch announces more layoffs. 7,000 jobs are currently at risk in Germany.
  • 03/11/2025 - The long-struggling Volkswagen software subsidiary Cariad plans to cut 1,600 jobs by the end of the year.
  • 03/17/2025 - Audi will cut a total of 7,500 jobs in Germany by the end of 2029.
  • 03/18/2025 - Siemens plans to cut around 6,000 jobs worldwide, 2,850 of them in Germany.

The list goes on and gets longer on a daily basis. However, a shakeup of this size should have been expected the moment the push of the electric mobility gained momentum. EVs don't need as many mechanical parts as internal combustion engine vehicles [ICE]. The design and assembly processes are significantly different, which implies changes in the production and supply. Unfortunately, the low response speed of German enterprises has led to an accumulation of drastic measures in the sector.

Volkswagen's Valuation

Volkswagen's valuation multiples and financial metrics don't look exciting. Based on the historical numbers from ten or more years in the past, there is no reason to think that the company is undervalued or worth investing in now with the hope of enjoying market-beating returns.

  • The P/E ratio has been at the current level for more than a decade, excluding a few short-lived spikes. Although it may appear low at 4.79, it's nothing extraordinary in the automotive sector.

  • The number of shares outstanding has increased by roughly 25% over the last 15 years, diluting shareholders.

  • Volkswagen, just like every automaker, suffers from minuscule operating and profit margins. In the case of the German manufacturer, these have always been at around 5.0% and 3.0% respectively.

  • Profitability metrics are weak, with the most important Return on Invested Capital [ROIC] at 2.96%. The number shows how effective the company is at generating profit from the capital invested in the business. The reason why ROIC is of significant importance when valuing a company was well explained by Charlie Munger, the former vice chairman of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), (BRK.B).

Over the long term, it’s hard for a stock to earn a much better return than the business which underlies it earns on capital.

  • Another disappointing multiple is Volkswagen's Free Cash Flow [FCF] per share generation over the years.

It's almost impossible to find any signs of a good business to invest in. The charts tell a story that corresponds with the previous article sections. They reflect the EU and its business-unfriendly policies, Germany with influential auto worker unions, and Volkswagen in between with tied hands and reduced autonomy.

Opportunities

The German Automaker will navigate the crisis the best it can. It has competent management and still the size, scale, and a strong, respected brand. Volkswagen will be trying to keep up with its domestic rivals and competitors from abroad.

The company announced a new affordable electric car ID. Every1 for around €20,000. The vehicle would complete a wide palette of the EVs offered by the German carmaker. This may help sales grow and spark some excitement around the Volkswagen brand.

Currently, tariffs on China-made EVs imported to the EU are in place at the 10.0% mark. Additionally, after an anti-subsidy investigation, new tariffs were imposed, amounting to 17.0% for BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY), 18.8% for Geely (OTCPK:VOLAF), and 35.3% for SAIC. The newly formed German government doesn't express an interest in lifting these duties. As long as they are in place, customers in the EU will be less willing to purchase a vehicle made in China, which gives a temporary advantage to domestic producers.

Changes in the auto sector are dynamic and can lead to unexpected results. It may be that Volkswagen will go through the crisis by slowly reducing its workforce, downsizing bureaucracy, and optimizing processes. While working on its EV palette and simultaneously improving its operations, it may end up stronger and better equipped for a fight for dominance in the global automotive industry.

The company laid out a strategy that is supposed to help the automaker attract customers, unleash its full potential, or reduce operating expenses. AI opens new ways to innovate and enhance the company's image as more attractive for highly skilled individuals who would join the Volkswagen workforce. Besides that, it's not the first time the business is going through a recession. In the past, it always managed to stay afloat and find ways to stay competitive. Volkswagen has also been ranked in the TOP 3 in terms of the number of vehicles produced for the last 18 years. This gives hope that this time won't be different, and the recession won't break the leading German automaker.

Conclusion

Volkswagen, just like any carmaker, isn't a great business. Fixed costs are high, no matter what the company does. This puts pressure on margins. Besides that, domestic and global policies have a strong influence on the company's operations. While the homeland of the automobile is experiencing a crisis, the company's fundamentals show multiple red flags to potential long-term investors. The situation Volkswagen faces is rather gloomy, and future perspectives aren't particularly promising. The business offers neither growth nor ample returns for its shareholders. Avoiding the stock and continuing the search for a great, undervalued business is the best a long-term investor can do.
